title: Frailty Assessment Tools Compared: Which One Should You Use?
description: Compare frailty assessment tools: Fried phenotype, Rockwood CFS, PRISMA-7, and Edmonton Frail Scale. Choose the right one.

blocks.question: Which frailty assessment tool is best for primary care?
blocks.directAnswer: No single 'best' tool. Fried phenotype (5 criteria including grip) is most studied and predictive of mortality. Rockwood Clinical Frailty Scale (CFS) is quick (1 minute). PRISMA-7 requires 7 yes-no questions. Edmonton Frail Scale adds cognition and mood. Choose based on clinical setting, time available, and population age.

tldrBullets.text: Fried phenotype: most validated, requires dynamometer and timed walk
Rockwood CFS: fastest (1 minute), uses clinical judgment and trajectory
PRISMA-7: simple, quick, no equipment, good rule-out tool

blocks.expandedAnswer:
Frailty is a state of vulnerability to adverse outcomes. It differs from disability and comorbidity. No universal consensus exists on optimal assessment method, creating variation in clinical practice.

The four most-validated tools are: Fried Phenotype (objective, 5 criteria), Rockwood Clinical Frailty Scale (subjective, 9-point ordinal scale), PRISMA-7 (7 yes-no questions), and Edmonton Frail Scale (includes cognition and mood). Each has trade-offs between simplicity, predictive accuracy, and clinical utility.

Frailty prevalence in community-dwelling 65-80 year-olds: 10-15%. In acute hospital settings: 40-50%. In long-term care: 60-80%. Early identification enables preventive interventions (nutrition, exercise, deprescribing) that slow or reverse frailty trajectory. Choice of tool depends on setting, time constraints, and available equipment.

sources.title: Comparison of frailty assessment tools in older adults
Fried phenotype criteria: longitudinal validation and mortality prediction
Rockwood Clinical Frailty Scale: validation and utility in clinical practice

blocks.body:
Why Frailty Matters: Recognition and Stratification

Frailty is distinct from aging, disability, and comorbidity. A 75-year-old with multiple conditions (diabetes, hypertension, heart failure) is NOT necessarily frail if reserve and resilience remain. Conversely, a 70-year-old with minimal disease burden may be frail and vulnerable.

Clinical consequences of undetected frailty:

  • Unexpected adverse events after minor surgery or acute illness
  • Poor recovery trajectory post-hospitalization
  • Falls and fractures from minimal trauma
  • Rapid functional decline and loss of independence
  • Mortality risk 2-4x higher than robust peers

Early recognition enables targeted intervention: strength training, optimized nutrition, medication review, cognitive engagement, and social connection. These modify frailty trajectory and maintain independence longer.

Tool-by-Tool Comparison: Criteria, Time, and Validation

Fried Phenotype (2001)

  • Criteria (any 3+ = frailty): Unintentional weight loss (>5% in year), exhaustion (self-reported), low physical activity, slow gait speed (<0.8 m/s on 15-foot walk), weak grip (<sex/BMI-adjusted cutoffs)
  • Time required: 10-15 minutes (includes timed walk, grip test)
  • Equipment: Hand dynamometer, stopwatch, 15-foot walkway
  • Validation: Prospective follow-up of 5,317 women (Cardiovascular Health Study). Each frailty criterion independently predicted mortality. Frail cohort (3+) had HR 5.3 for mortality over 3 years.
  • Strengths: Objective, reproducible, excellent outcome prediction
  • Limitations: Time-consuming, requires equipment and space

Rockwood Clinical Frailty Scale (2005)

  • Criteria: 9-point ordinal scale (1=very fit, 9=terminally ill). Based on clinical impression, medical complexity, comorbidity, and functional decline trajectory
  • Time required: 1 minute (clinical judgment only)
  • Equipment: None
  • Validation: Tested in >50 cohorts. Predicts hospitalization, long-term care admission, and mortality. CFS >5 = increased risk. Good inter-rater reliability with training.
  • Strengths: Fastest tool, integrates clinical trajectory, validated globally
  • Limitations: Subjective, requires clinical experience, variable inter-rater reliability without training

PRISMA-7 (2010)

  • Criteria: 7 yes-no questions: age >85, male sex, living in institution, polymedication (>4 drugs), self-reported poor health, help with IADL (instrumental activities of daily living), loss of self-reliance. Score 2+ = at-risk
  • Time required: 3-5 minutes
  • Equipment: None
  • Validation: Designed for primary care. Sensitivity 80% for frailty-related adverse events (hospitalization, falls, mortality). Less specific than Fried; good rule-out tool.
  • Strengths: Simple, no equipment, pragmatic for busy practice
  • Limitations: Less precise than Fried, includes non-frailty factors (age, drugs, living situation)

Edmonton Frail Scale (2007)

  • Criteria: 11 items including grip strength, timed chair stand, balance, cognition (clock draw), mood (GDS), medication use. Total 0-17 score (>7=frail)
  • Time required: 5-7 minutes
  • Equipment: Chair, stopwatch, hand dynamometer, clock paper
  • Validation: Canadian study (600 older adults). Predicts falls, hospitalization, and mortality. Unique inclusion of cognition and mood improves detection of vulnerable older adults.
  • Strengths: Comprehensive (captures physical, cognitive, mood domains), good outcome prediction
  • Limitations: Requires training and equipment, longer than Rockwood

Choosing the Right Tool: Clinical Context Guide

Primary care office (time-limited): Use Rockwood CFS (1 minute) or PRISMA-7 (5 minutes). Both identify at-risk patients without equipment. Follow positive screens with detailed assessment if managing complex patient.

Hospital admission or acute care: Use Fried Phenotype or Edmonton Frail Scale if patient able to participate in physical testing. Both predict post-discharge adverse events. For patients too unwell to perform testing, use Rockwood CFS as clinical impression during admission assessment.

Research or clinical trial: Fried Phenotype is gold standard. Objective criteria and extensive validation literature. EWGSOP2 sarcopenia algorithm (grip + gait + muscle mass) complements Fried for mechanistic understanding.

Community screening (seniors' programs, falls clinics): Start with PRISMA-7 or Rockwood CFS to identify high-risk cohort, then perform Fried or Edmonton on positive screens for intervention targeting.

Long-term care or post-acute facility: Edmonton Frail Scale is preferred. Captures cognition and mood common in this population. Able to be administered by nursing staff with training.

stats.stat: Fried frailty (3+ criteria) predicts HR 5.3 mortality over 3 years
Rockwood CFS score >5 predicts hospitalization and mortality
PRISMA-7 sensitivity 80% for frailty-related adverse events

items.question: Can I use multiple frailty tools in the same patient for better accuracy?
items.answer: Yes, this strengthens risk assessment. Example: screen with PRISMA-7 or Rockwood CFS, confirm with Fried if eligible for testing. If concordant (both identify frailty), risk is high. If discordant, clinical judgment needed. Adding grip strength + gait speed to any tool improves outcome prediction.

items.question: How often should frailty be reassessed in a managed patient?
items.answer: Baseline assessment on first contact. In stable community-dwelling older adults, reassess annually. In acute hospitalization or post-acute care, reassess monthly (trajectory matters). If frail at baseline, monthly assessment during intervention (exercise, nutrition, deprescribing) tracks response. If improving, move to quarterly monitoring.

items.question: Does frailty score predict which interventions will help?
items.answer: Partially. Fried phenotype components suggest intervention targets: low activity warrants exercise, weight loss warrants nutrition review, exhaustion warrants depression screening or sleep assessment. Rockwood CFS indicates clinical complexity but does not specify interventions. Combine CFS clinical impression with Fried component analysis for targeted approach.

items.question: Can pre-frail status (intermediate frailty) be reversed?
items.answer: Yes. Pre-frail individuals (Fried 1-2 criteria) respond well to targeted interventions. Randomized trials show resistance training (2-3 times weekly) plus protein (1.6 g/kg) reverses pre-frailty in 12 weeks. Cognitive engagement and social connection slow progression. Early intervention in pre-frail stage has highest success rate.

items.question: How does the EWGSOP2 sarcopenia algorithm relate to frailty assessment?
items.answer: They are complementary, not redundant. EWGSOP2 (grip + gait + muscle mass) diagnoses sarcopenia specifically. Frailty assessment (Fried, Rockwood, etc.) captures broader domains (energy, activity, medical complexity). Many frail patients are sarcopenic, but not all. Use both: frailty tool identifies at-risk patient, then EWGSOP2 algorithm clarifies if sarcopenia is driving problem.

blocks.body:
Interventions for Frailty Reversal: Where Evidence Is Strong

Frailty is partially reversible, particularly in pre-frail stage (1-2 Fried criteria positive). Key interventions:

  • Resistance and balance training: 2-3 sessions weekly for 12+ weeks reverses gait slowness and improves grip. Expected gains: 0.1-0.2 m/s gait speed, 2-4 kg grip, 1-2 more 30-second chair stand repetitions.
  • Protein-enriched nutrition: 1.6 g/kg daily (higher in catabolic states like cancer or post-acute care) supports muscle adaptation to training and stabilizes weight.
  • Cognitive engagement: Brain training, education, social engagement reduce frailty progression. Mechanism unclear; likely involves stress reduction and preserved neuroplasticity.
  • Deprescribing: Polypharmacy (>10 medications) associates with frailty. Careful medication review (stopping non-essential drugs, simplifying regimens) improves energy and motivation for self-care.

Multicomponent programs (exercise plus nutrition plus cognitive engagement plus medication review) show 30-50% reduction in frailty progression over 12 months. Single-component interventions are less effective.

Able Assess as Frailty Screening Component

Able Assess automates grip strength and timed-walk testing, two core frailty components. Objective measurement reduces inter-rater variation and enables standardized serial tracking. Results integrate into clinical workflow, automatically flagging patients meeting Fried criteria or showing rapid decline trajectory requiring escalation.

Able Assess is not a standalone frailty diagnostic tool, but materially accelerates the assessment process. Practitioners retain responsibility for applying clinical judgment using Rockwood CFS or Fried criteria frameworks. By automating objective components (grip strength plus gait speed), clinicians gain time to focus on subjective elements (exhaustion and fatigue history, unintentional weight loss timeline, activity level patterns, medical complexity review) that require human assessment and cannot be automated.

Integration benefits: serial grip and gait data enable trajectory analysis identifying patients at inflection point (accelerating decline). Population-level reporting flags cohorts with highest frailty prevalence (long-term care facilities show 60-80% frail on Edmonton or Fried measures). Risk stratification informs resource allocation and preventive program design. Longitudinal tracking across annual or biennial assessments reveals which patients respond to intervention versus declining despite support, guiding escalation or palliative approach.

blocks.heading: Integrate frailty screening into your clinical workflow
blocks.bodyText: Choose the right tool for your setting. Able Assess provides objective grip and gait data to support Fried or other frailty frameworks.
